body.p: If you want to maintain a larger MarkLogic application, you will not be able to avoid a build / deployment managerDeployment-Manager. Typically, it is necessary to maintain a development server, a test server, and a production server.Tools for MarkLogicmlproj

ml-gradle Project page on GitHub for ml-gradle and ml-proj Website of the mlproj project are such tools. One is based on the popular Java build chain gradle Gradle, the other is a NodeJS application by the same maintainer as the previously described EXPath Console.
As I was able to find out during a larger evaluation task, both tools are better suited for projects that are started from scratch, because they rely on naming conventions in the file structure.
Both claim (almost) full configurability of the project structure, however, this comes with several pitfalls, so that I unfortunately cannot recommend migrating an existing project.

section.title: ml-gradle
section.p: ml-gradle is based on the popular Java build tool ml-gradle Project page on GitHub for ml-gradle Tools for MarkLogicml-gradle . Using predefined or custom tasks, JSON files are automatically created that serve as payload for REST calls to MarkLogic. The MarkLogic REST API MarkLogic REST API is used for this purpose. During the deployment action itself, the payload is then collected and submitted.
In principle, this idea is not bad, but there are now several places where the configuration can be modified - which could of course have a negative effect if the developers lack discipline:

li.p: Setting predefined properties Properties for the MarkLogic deployment tool ml-gradle in the file gradle.properties
Using predefined create tasks Documentation page for ml-gradle regarding tasks for creating new resources, e.g. gradle mlNewDatabase
skeleton files are generated, which are then manually completed with missing configuration information.
There is a token-based mechanism ml-gradle config, with which predefined placeholders in configuration files (payload files) are dynamically replaced.

Custom Groovy tasks can be implemented. These are integrated, for example, in the file build.gradle. Example of a simple Groovy task Example of a simple Groovy task on the ml-gradle documentation page

section.title: mlproj
section.p: mlproj is a NodeJS application by Florent Georges Florent Georges. As with ml-gradle, I would not recommend migrating an existing project structure to mlproj, but rather starting a project from scratch with it.

While ml-gradle is about keeping track of multiple, partially automatically generated configuration files and options, the configuration in mlproj takes place in just one file prod.json or dev.json, which inherits or overrides the settings of a base configuration base.json.

Of course, the extension mechanism is not quite as powerful as with ml-gradle - after all, it lacks a full-fledged build chain as a foundation. However, a NodeJS developer will work well with it. The source code appears tidy and the project is well documented.

Both tools have a "watch" mode, in which the developer's file structure is monitored. As soon as a file changes, it is uploaded to MarkLogic. As an example of the extensive configuration options, the docstring of the "watch" mode in mlproj is shown below:
section.pre:
mlproj watch [-a srv|-b db] [-s src|-/ dir|-1 file] [what]
-a, --as, --server <srv> server, get its modules database -b, --db, --database <db> target database -B, --sys, --system-database <db> the name of the target system db -s, --src, --source-set <dir> source set to watch -/, --dir, --directory <dir> directory to watch -1, --doc, --document <file> file to watch <what> source set or directory to watch
